concepts.lesson: The Cartesian coordinate system is formed by two perpendicular number lines called the X-axis and the Y-axis, which intersect at a point called the origin. The X-axis represents horizontal movement, while the Y-axis represents vertical movement. This system provides a precise way to locate points in a plane using numerical values. It forms the foundation of coordinate geometry and is widely used in mathematics, science, engineering, and real-life applications such as maps and graphs.
concepts.real_example: Locating a city on a map using east–west and north–south directions.

concepts.lesson: The position of a point in the Cartesian plane is represented by an ordered pair (x, y), where x is called the abscissa and y is called the ordinate. The order of the numbers is important because changing the order represents a different point. Coordinates describe both the direction and distance of a point from the origin, making them essential for plotting graphs and analysing geometric figures.
concepts.real_example: The point (3, 4) represents moving 3 units right and 4 units up.

concepts.lesson: The X-axis and Y-axis divide the coordinate plane into four regions called quadrants. Each quadrant has a specific sign pattern for x and y coordinates. Understanding quadrants helps in quickly identifying the location of a point and predicting the signs of its coordinates. Points lying exactly on the axes do not belong to any quadrant.
concepts.real_example: Temperature above or below zero combined with east or west direction.

concepts.lesson: A point that lies on the X-axis has its y-coordinate equal to zero, while a point that lies on the Y-axis has its x-coordinate equal to zero. Such points are important special cases because they simplify calculations and do not lie in any quadrant. Understanding points on axes is essential when analysing graphs and solving coordinate geometry problems.
concepts.real_example: A city located exactly on the equator or prime meridian.
key_points: Such points do not lie in any quadrant

concepts.concept_name: Distance of a Point from Axes
concepts.lesson: The distance of a point from the X-axis is given by the absolute value of its y-coordinate, and the distance from the Y-axis is given by the absolute value of its x-coordinate. Absolute value is used because distance is always non-negative, regardless of direction. This concept is useful in problems involving perpendicular distances, graphs, and real-life interpretations such as height or depth relative to a reference line.
concepts.real_example: Height of a building from ground level irrespective of direction.
